summary: Gruber reviews the Apple Watch Series 3 with cellular, arguing that cellular connectivity gives the watch something essential: independence from the iPhone. He frames this as perfectly targeted at the two things people already love about Apple Watch — notifications and fitness — since those are the exact moments you're separated from your phone. He criticizes carrier pricing at $10/month as too high and the red dot on the digital crown as an unnecessary aesthetic distraction. He also praises WatchOS 4's new list view for apps, calling the original honeycomb design one that 'works like shit,' and wishes for a camera, always-on display, and better haptics in future models.
key_points: Cellular networking gives Apple Watch its most important quality yet — independence from the iPhone — targeting the exact use cases where people are already separated from their phones

Apple managed to add cellular without making the watch thicker or heavier while maintaining all-day battery life, which reflects their early-product-line strategy of adding features before slimming down
The $10/month carrier fee is too high for a device used instead of an iPhone, not in addition to it — $5/month would be fair
The honeycomb app launcher is a bad design that WatchOS 4's new alphabetical list view finally fixes, and Apple should have made the list the default
The red dot on the digital crown is an aesthetic misstep that clashes with many watch bands and draws unnecessary attention
money_quotes: The answer, sometimes, is not when it's done, but rather when it's useful.
With the addition of cellular networking in Series 3, Apple Watch gains something essential: independence.
It is not a replacement for a phone, and is not supposed to be.
The effect of these performance improvements isn't that it makes Apple Watch Series 3 feel fast, but that it makes it feel not slow.
Apple turned Apple Watch into a goddamn cell phone, without making the device thicker or heavier, and it still lasts all day.
The honeycomb looks cool, especially when you pan around, but it works like shit, and it's a reminder of the unfocused nature of the original Apple Watch.
The 'home' screen on Apple Watch is your watch face.
People won't know you're calling them from your watch if you don't tell them.
key_insight: Cellular networking transforms Apple Watch from a tethered accessory into an independent device, perfectly targeting the moments — exercise, travel — when you're already separated from your phone.
